response: The EU’s General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR), in force since May 2018, requires any organization handling the personal data of EU residents to uphold rights of access, erasure, portability and obtain explicit consent, appoint Data Protection Officers for large-scale processing, and report breaches within 72 hours. Non-compliance can incur fines of up to 4 percent of global annual revenue or €20 million (whichever is higher), with recent penalties including €1.2 billion against Meta, €746 million against Amazon and over €200 million against Google. GDPR has influenced similar laws worldwide—such as California’s CCPA, Brazil’s LGPD and India’s Digital Personal Data Protection Act—but critics say it imposes heavy burdens on small businesses, leads to inconsistent enforcement and frustrates users with intrusive cookie banners. Despite these challenges, GDPR has established data privacy as a fundamental right and set the global standard for data protection.
